questions.question: Which systems of classification used only gross superficial morphological characters such as habit, colour, number and shape of leaves?
questions.explanation: The earliest systems of classification used only gross superficial morphological characters such as habit, colour, number and shape of leaves, etc. Such systems were artificial.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Who proposed the natural classification system for flowering plants based on natural affinities and internal features?
options: George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker
questions.explanation: Natural classification systems developed, which were based on natural affinities among the organisms... Such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Phylogenetic classification systems are primarily based on:
questions.explanation: At present phylogenetic classification systems based on evolutionary relationships between the various organisms are acceptable.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which taxonomic method is easily carried out using computers and is based on assigning numbers and codes to all observable characters?
questions.explanation: Numerical Taxonomy which is now easily carried out using computers is based on all observable characteristics. Number and codes are assigned to all the characters and the data are then processed.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Cytotaxonomy is based on which of the following information?
options: Cytological information like chromosome number, structure, and behaviour
questions.explanation: Cytotaxonomy... is based on cytological information like chromosome number, structure, behaviour.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which branch of taxonomy uses the chemical constituents of the plant to resolve confusions?
questions.explanation: ...and chemotaxonomy, that uses the chemical constituents of the plant to resolve confusions, are also used by taxonomists these days.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following describes the reproduction in algae where fusion occurs between two gametes dissimilar in size?
questions.explanation: Fusion of two gametes dissimilar in size, as in species of Eudorina is termed as anisogamous.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In oogamous reproduction, the fusing gametes consist of:
options: One large non-motile female gamete and a smaller motile male gamete
One small motile female gamete and a large non-motile male gamete
questions.explanation: Fusion between one large, non-motile (static) female gamete and a smaller, motile male gamete is termed oogamous, e.g., Volvox, Fucus.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Approximately what proportion of the total carbon dioxide fixation on earth is carried out by algae?
questions.explanation: At least a half of the total carbon dioxide fixation on earth is carried out by algae through photosynthesis.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following substances are hydrocolloids produced by marine algae?
questions.explanation: Certain marine brown and red algae produce large amounts of hydrocolloids... e.g., algin (brown algae) and carrageen (red algae) which are used commercially.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Agar, used to grow microbes and in the preparation of ice-creams, is obtained from:
questions.explanation: Agar, one of the commercial products obtained from Gelidium and Gracilaria are used to grow microbes and in preparations of ice-creams and jellies.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which unicellular alga is rich in proteins and used as a food supplement by space travellers?
questions.explanation: Chlorella, a unicellular alga rich in proteins, is used as food supplement even by space travellers.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Green algae (Chlorophyceae) are usually grass green due to the dominance of which pigments?
questions.explanation: They are usually grass green due to the dominance of pigments chlorophyll a and b.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What are the storage bodies located in the chloroplasts of most green algae called?
questions.explanation: Most of the members have one or more storage bodies called pyrenoids located in the chloroplasts.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What do pyrenoids in green algae primarily contain besides starch?
questions.explanation: Pyrenoids contain protein besides starch.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The rigid cell wall of green algae is composed of:
options: An inner layer of pectose and an outer layer of cellulose
An inner layer of cellulose and an outer layer of pectose
Cellulose covered by a gelatinous coating of algin
questions.explanation: Green algae usually have a rigid cell wall made of an inner layer of cellulose and an outer layer of pectose.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a profusely branched brown alga that may reach a height of 100 metres?
questions.explanation: They range from simple branched, filamentous forms (Ectocarpus) to profusely branched forms as represented by kelps, which may reach a height of 100 metres.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which specific xanthophyll pigment primarily determines the varying shades of colour in brown algae?
questions.explanation: They vary in colour from olive green to various shades of brown depending upon the amount of the xanthophyll pigment, fucoxanthin present in them.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How is food typically stored in Phaeophyceae (brown algae)?
options: As complex carbohydrates like laminarin or mannitol
questions.explanation: Food is stored as complex carbohydrates, which may be in the form of laminarin or mannitol.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The vegetative cells of brown algae have a cellulosic wall usually covered on the outside by a gelatinous coating of:
questions.explanation: The vegetative cells have a cellulosic wall usually covered on the outside by a gelatinous coating of algin.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The plant body of a brown alga is usually attached to the substratum by a:
questions.explanation: The plant body is usually attached to the substratum by a holdfast, and has a stalk, the stipe and leaf like photosynthetic organ the frond.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the characteristic of zoospores in most brown algae?
options: They are pear-shaped (pyriform) and bear two laterally attached flagella
They have a single apical flagellum
They are identical in size to female gametes
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction in most brown algae is by biflagellate zoospores that are pear-shaped and have two unequal laterally attached flagella.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Members of Rhodophyceae are commonly called red algae due to the predominance of which pigment?
questions.explanation: The members of rhodophyceae are commonly called red algae because of the predominance of the red pigment, r-phycoerythrin in their body.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In red algae, food is stored in the form of:
questions.explanation: The food is stored as floridean starch which is very similar to amylopectin and glycogen in structure.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following algae reproduce asexually by non-motile spores and sexually by non-motile gametes?
questions.explanation: The red algae usually reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation. They reproduce asexually by non-motile spores and sexually by non-motile gametes.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which plants are referred to as the 'amphibians of the plant kingdom'?
questions.explanation: Bryophytes are also called amphibians of the plant kingdom because these plants can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The main plant body of a bryophyte is:
questions.explanation: The main plant body of the bryophyte is haploid. It produces gametes, hence is called a gametophyte.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In bryophytes, the female sex organ is flask-shaped and is called:
questions.explanation: The female sex organ called archegonium is flask-shaped and produces a single egg.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which moss provides peat that has long been used as fuel and packing material?
questions.explanation: Species of Sphagnum, a moss, provide peat that have long been used as fuel, and as packing material...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Along with lichens, which organisms are the first to colonise rocks and decompose them?
questions.explanation: Mosses along with lichens are the first organisms to colonise rocks and hence, are of great ecological importance. They decompose rocks...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In liverworts, asexual reproduction takes place by the formation of green, multicellular, asexual buds called:
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction in liverworts takes place by fragmentation of thalli, or by the formation of specialised structures called gemmae.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the first stage of the moss gametophyte that develops directly from a spore?
questions.explanation: The first stage is the protonema stage, which develops directly from a spore. It is a creeping, green, branched and frequently filamentous stage.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The sporophyte in bryophytes is differentiated into:
questions.explanation: The sporophyte is differentiated into a foot, seta and capsule. After meiosis, spores are produced within the capsule.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Evolutionarily, which group of plants are the first terrestrial plants to possess vascular tissues (xylem and phloem)?
questions.explanation: The Pteridophytes... Evolutionarily, they are the first terrestrial plants to possess vascular tissues - xylem and phloem.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In pteridophytes, the dominant phase in the life cycle is the:
questions.explanation: However, in pteridophytes, the main plant body is a sporophyte which is differentiated into true root, stem and leaves.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The inconspicuous, small but multicellular, free-living thalloid gametophyte in pteridophytes is called:
questions.explanation: The spores germinate to give rise to inconspicuous, small but multicellular, free-living, mostly photosynthetic thalloid gametophytes called prothallus.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is a pteridophyte with small leaves (microphylls)?
questions.explanation: The leaves in pteridophyta are small (microphylls) as in Selaginella or large (macrophylls) as in ferns.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In some pteridophytes, sporophylls form distinct compact structures called:
questions.explanation: In some cases sporophylls may form distinct compact structures called strobili or cones (Selaginella, Equisetum).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Pteridophytes like Selaginella and Salvinia that produce macro and micro spores are known as:
questions.explanation: Genera like Selaginella and Salvinia which produce two kinds of spores, macro (large) and micro (small) spores, are known as heterosporous.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In heterosporous pteridophytes, the development of zygotes into young embryos within the female gametophyte is considered a precursor to the:
questions.explanation: The development of the zygotes into young embryos take place within the female gametophytes. This event is a precursor to the seed habit considered an important step in evolution.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Equisetum belongs to which class of pteridophytes?
questions.explanation: The pteridophytes are further classified into four classes: Psilopsida... Lycopsida... Sphenopsida (Equisetum) and Pteropsida...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the defining characteristic of gymnosperms regarding their ovules?
options: They are enclosed within a fruit
They are not enclosed by any ovary wall and remain exposed
They are produced on the leaves of a prothallus
They are dispersed by water only
questions.explanation: The gymnosperms (gymnos: naked, sperma: seeds) are plants in which the ovules are not enclosed by any ovary wall and remain exposed, both before and after fertilisation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Roots of which gymnosperm genus have a fungal association in the form of mycorrhiza?
questions.explanation: Roots in some genera have fungal association in the form of mycorrhiza (Pinus)...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Small specialised roots associated with N2-fixing cyanobacteria in Cycas are called:
questions.explanation: ...while in some others (Cycas) small specialised roots called coralloid roots are associated with N2- fixing cyanobacteria.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following adaptations help conifer leaves reduce water loss?
options: Broad surface area and thin cuticle
Needle-like shape, thick cuticle, and sunken stomata
Loss of all leaves during winter
Presence of numerous large stomata on the upper surface
questions.explanation: In conifers, the needle-like leaves reduce the surface area. Their thick cuticle and sunken stomata also help to reduce water loss.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In gymnosperms, the highly reduced male gametophyte is called a:
questions.explanation: The microspores develop into a male gametophytic generation which is highly reduced... This reduced gametophyte is called a pollen grain.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In Cycas, how are the male cones and megasporophylls borne?
questions.explanation: However, in cycas male cones and megasporophylls are borne on different trees.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which statement is true regarding the gametophytes of gymnosperms compared to bryophytes and pteridophytes?
options: They are the dominant phase of the life cycle
They have an independent free-living existence
They do not have an independent free-living existence
questions.explanation: Unlike bryophytes and pteridophytes, in gymnosperms, the male and the female gametophytes do not have an independent free-living existence. They remain within the sporangia retained on the sporophytes.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In which group of plants are pollen grains and ovules developed in specialised structures called flowers, and seeds enclosed in fruits?
questions.explanation: Unlike the gymnosperms where the ovules are naked, in the angiosperms or flowering plants, the pollen grains and ovules are developed in specialised structures called flowers. In angiosperms, the seeds are enclosed in fruits.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the smallest known angiosperm?
questions.explanation: They range in size from the smallest Wolffia to tall trees of Eucalyptus (over 100 metres).
questions.subject: Biology
